policy.title: RBI finalizes Climate Risk Disclosure Framework for Scheduled Commercial Banks
policy.description: RBI released final guidelines mandating climate risk disclosures for all SCBs with assets above ₹50,000 crore, effective FY2027-28. Banks must report physical and transition risk exposures using standardized templates aligned with ISSB S2.

policy.title: SEBI expands BRSR Core to top 500 listed companies with assurance mandate
policy.description: SEBI extended mandatory BRSR Core reporting with reasonable assurance to top 500 companies by market cap from FY2026-27, adding Scope 3 emissions and water stress metrics. Limited assurance requirement now covers all 1000 BRSR filers.

policy.title: IRDAI issues draft guidelines for parametric insurance products
policy.description: IRDAI released exposure draft permitting insurers to offer parametric covers for agriculture, SME, and property segments with defined trigger mechanisms. Guidelines specify minimum data standards for index-based payouts and require RBI-approved weather data providers.

policy.title: Basel Committee publishes final Pillar 3 climate disclosure requirements
policy.description: BCBS finalized Pillar 3 climate disclosure framework with implementation from January 2028. Banks must disclose climate-sensitive sector exposures, physical risk concentrations by geography, and transition risk metrics including financed emissions.

policy.title: European Commission adopts Taxonomy criteria for remaining environmental objectives
policy.description: EC finalized technical screening criteria for water, circular economy, pollution, and biodiversity objectives under EU Taxonomy. Financial institutions must report Taxonomy alignment for these objectives from FY2027 reporting.

policy.title: NGFS releases Phase V climate scenarios with enhanced physical risk granularity
policy.description: Updated NGFS scenarios now include country-level physical risk damage functions and sector-specific transition pathways for emerging markets. New scenarios model chronic physical risks through 2100 with GDP impact estimates.

competitors.title: Jupiter Intelligence partners with HDFC Bank for physical risk assessment
competitors.description: Jupiter announced a multi-year engagement with HDFC Bank to provide physical climate risk scoring for the bank's commercial real estate and infrastructure portfolio. The partnership covers flood, cyclone, and heat stress hazards across 15 Indian states.

competitors.title: MSCI launches Climate Value-at-Risk module for Indian corporate bonds
competitors.description: MSCI expanded its Climate VaR coverage to 450 Indian corporate bond issuers, providing physical and transition risk metrics aligned with RBI's draft climate framework. The module integrates with MSCI's existing ESG ratings platform.

competitors.title: Sprih raises $12M Series A for ESG compliance platform expansion
competitors.description: Bangalore-based Sprih closed Series A led by Sequoia India to expand its automated BRSR reporting platform. The company plans to add climate risk modules and target mid-market NBFCs and infrastructure companies.

competitors.title: XDI releases asset-level physical risk API for South Asian markets
competitors.description: XDI launched granular physical risk analytics covering 8 hazards across India, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka at asset-level resolution. The API provides damage probability curves and financial loss estimates for individual properties.

competitors.title: Moody's partners with CRISIL for integrated ESG-credit risk scores
competitors.description: Moody's and CRISIL announced joint development of ESG-adjusted credit risk indicators for Indian corporates. The product combines CRISIL's credit expertise with Moody's global ESG frameworks for bank lending decisions.

competitors.title: Watershed raises $200M Series D, announces enterprise climate platform
competitors.description: Watershed closed $200M at $3.5B valuation and launched integrated platform combining carbon accounting with physical risk assessment. The company announced expansion into APAC with offices in Singapore and Mumbai.

research.title: Climate Risk and Financial Stability in Emerging Markets: Evidence from Bank Stress Tests
research.description: BIS analysis of 12 emerging market climate stress tests found median bank capital shortfalls of 1.2-2.8% under severe physical risk scenarios. The study highlights data gaps in asset-level hazard exposure as the primary obstacle to accurate risk quantification.

research.title: Scaling Parametric Insurance in Climate-Vulnerable Economies
research.description: Swiss Re estimates parametric insurance penetration in South Asia could reach $8B by 2030, up from $1.2B in 2025. The report identifies index design quality and counterparty trust as critical barriers, recommending bank-insurer partnerships for distribution.

research.title: Physical Climate Risk Assessment for Indian Banking Sector
research.description: RBI's pilot study across 15 PSU and private banks found 23% of agricultural loan portfolios exposed to high physical risk from flood and drought. The report recommends banks develop sector-specific PD adjustment factors for climate-sensitive exposures.

research.title: ESG Data Availability in Emerging Markets: Gaps and Solutions
research.description: IFC assessment found only 18% of listed companies in South Asia provide decision-useful climate data, compared to 67% in developed markets. The report calls for alternative data approaches including satellite imagery and supply chain proxies.

research.title: Integrating Climate Risk into Credit Risk Models: A Framework for Banks
research.description: UNEP FI framework provides methodologies for adjusting PD and LGD parameters based on physical and transition risk factors. Pilot results with 8 banks showed climate-adjusted PDs 15-40% higher than baseline for high-exposure sectors.

research.title: Weather Index Insurance: Lessons from a Decade of Implementation
research.description: World Bank review of 45 parametric programs found basis risk averaging 12-18% in well-designed products. Success factors include high-resolution weather data, transparent trigger mechanisms, and integration with existing financial services.

ourRead.headline: RBI's finalized climate disclosure framework creates an immediate addressable market while global players accelerate their India entry.
body: This week's signals converge on a single theme: India's climate risk infrastructure is institutionalizing rapidly, but incumbents and global entrants are moving to capture the opportunity before domestic specialists can scale. The regulatory runway that StepChange anticipated is now a live market—with competition arriving faster than expected.

The RBI's finalized climate risk disclosure framework (policy-1) is the most consequential development. With mandatory physical and transition risk reporting for banks above ₹50,000 crore from FY2027-28, approximately 40 scheduled commercial banks now face hard deadlines for capabilities most don't possess internally. The explicit alignment with ISSB S2 and the RBI pilot study showing 23% agricultural portfolio exposure (research-3) create direct demand for StepChange's PD/LGD adjustment models. IRDAI's parametric insurance draft (policy-3) simultaneously validates Bet #2, though the requirement for RBI-approved weather data providers introduces a potential gatekeeping risk StepChange must monitor.

The competitive landscape is intensifying faster than comfortable. Jupiter Intelligence's HDFC Bank partnership (comp-1) represents the first major global climate risk player securing a top-3 Indian private bank—territory StepChange should have locked down. XDI's South Asia API launch (comp-4) and MSCI's India-specific Climate VaR module (comp-2) show global players treating India as a priority market, not an afterthought. The Moody's-CRISIL partnership (comp-5) is particularly threatening: it combines global methodology credibility with local credit expertise, precisely the positioning StepChange claims. Domestically, Sprih's $12M raise signals investor confidence in the compliance layer, though their planned climate risk expansion could create a bundled competitor.

The BIS and UNEP FI research (research-1, research-5) provide immediate product validation: BIS identifies asset-level hazard data as the primary bottleneck for EM climate stress tests, exactly what StepChange's physical risk intelligence solves, while UNEP FI's framework showing 15-40% PD uplift for high-exposure sectors gives banks concrete evidence that climate adjustment matters. The IFC data availability study (research-4) reinforces Bet #3's thesis but also highlights the scale of the alternative data challenge—18% coverage means 82% requires proxy methodologies StepChange must prove are credible.

Watch: IRDAI's final parametric guidelines and which weather data providers receive approval—this could either unlock or constrain Bet #2. Action: Accelerate bank partnerships before Jupiter and XDI capture more marquee logos. The window between regulatory mandate and market saturation is 18-24 months; StepChange needs 3-5 signed PSU bank contracts in Q2 to establish defensible positioning.
